blurb: Bright lupine-like spikes of golden pea flowers light up late spring over clean blue-green trifoliate foliage — a vigorous, deer-proof native that spreads into bold drifts.
water: Low to moderate; xeric once established
seasons: Golden flower spikes late spring–early summer; blue-green trifoliate foliage; curved seed pods in summer
wildlife: Bumblebees and native bees; larval host for sulphur butterflies
toxic: Foliage and seeds contain quinolizidine alkaloids — toxic if eaten; keep from children, pets, and livestock.

care.hardiness: A tough Rocky Mountain native of the foothills, mountains, and plains, fully hardy through Boulder's zone 5b–6a winters; it grows wild at 6,000–9,500 ft in the region.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant in spring after the mid-May last frost or in early fall, in well-drained soil. Site it where it can spread, as it runs underground; in containers use a gritty mix and keep it lean and dry. Once established it needs little attention.[1]
care.sun: Full sun to part sun; it blooms best in open sun but tolerates light shade at woodland edges.[1]
care.soil: Rocky, sandy, well-drained soil; as a legume it fixes its own nitrogen and wants lean, not rich, ground. It adapts to Front-Range conditions but needs sharp drainage.[1]
care.water: Very low to low; drought-tolerant once established, needing little supplemental water in our semi-arid climate.[1]
care.spacing: Give it room — it spreads underground into colonies, so space ~18–24 inches and expect it to fill in.[1]
care.propagation: By seed or division of the spreading rhizomes. As a hard-coated legume seed, scarification (and a cold-moist stratification) improves germination; established colonies can be divided since it spreads by underground stems.[1]
care.stratify: Hard legume seed benefits from scarification plus a cold-moist stratification to break dormancy.[1]
care.bloom: Bright yellow, lupine-like pea flowers in spring to early summer — roughly April through June (sometimes into July at elevation).[1][2]
care.maintenance: Low; let it naturalize, and cut back after bloom if you want to tidy it. It can spread aggressively by rhizome, so site accordingly.[1]
care.selfsow: Spreads by both seed and creeping rhizomes into colonies, so give it space or contain it.[1]
care.troubles: Few pests thanks to its toxic alkaloids, which deter browsing; the main 'problem' is its vigorous underground spread.[1][3]

edible.summary: Poisonous — all parts contain lupine-like quinolizidine alkaloids; do not eat any part.[3]
edible.caution: Golden banner contains quinolizidine alkaloids (anagyrine, thermopsine, cytisine, N-methylcytisine), the same alkaloid class that makes lupines toxic, with the highest concentration in the seeds and flowers. The pea-like green pods and 'beans' are the real danger: children have been poisoned after eating the immature seeds mistaken for edible peas, developing headache, vomiting, dizziness, and abdominal pain. It is also hazardous to pets and livestock — these alkaloids deter browsing. Keep seeds and foliage away from children and animals; do not eat any part.[3]
